subject: History
chapter_name: Why had international peace collapsed by 1939?

note_data.title: Why had international peace collapsed by 1939?
keyPoints: **Hitler's foreign policy** aimed to destroy the Treaty of Versailles and create a Greater German Reich
**Rearmament** began secretly in 1933, openly announced in 1935, breaking the Treaty of Versailles
**Remilitarisation of the Rhineland** (1936) was Hitler's first major gamble that succeeded due to French and British inaction
**Anschluss with Austria** (1938) was achieved through intimidation and invasion, further strengthening Germany
**Appeasement policy** by Britain and France encouraged Hitler by giving him what he wanted to avoid war
**Munich Agreement** (1938) gave Hitler the Sudetenland without consulting Czechoslovakia
**Nazi-Soviet Pact** (August 1939) removed the threat of a two-front war for Germany
**Invasion of Poland** (September 1939) finally forced Britain and France to declare war
**Failure of the League of Nations** in Manchuria and Abyssinia showed collective security was dead

definitions.definition: The policy of giving in to the demands of an aggressive power in order to avoid war
The union of Germany and Austria, forbidden by the Treaty of Versailles but achieved by Hitler in 1938
Living space - Hitler's policy of expanding German territory to provide land for the German people
The German-speaking border areas of Czechoslovakia given to Hitler at Munich in 1938

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give arguments for AND against, then reach a balanced judgement

modelAnswer.question: Explain why the policy of appeasement failed to prevent war. [6 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Appeasement failed because it encouraged Hitler to make greater demands. When Britain and France gave Hitler the Sudetenland at Munich, this convinced him they would not fight, so he demanded more territory. Appeasement also made Germany stronger because each success gave Hitler more resources and confidence. The Anschluss with Austria gave Germany more soldiers and weapons. Finally, appeasement destroyed the collective security system because it showed the democracies would not work together to stop aggression, encouraging dictators like Hitler to take greater risks.
markBreakdown: Mark 1-2: Encouraged Hitler to make greater demands
Mark 3-4: Made Germany stronger militarily and economically
Mark 5-6: Destroyed collective security and encouraged other aggressors

commonMistakes: Never say Hitler 'wanted world war' - he wanted to expand Germany but hoped to avoid a major war
Students often confuse the Saar plebiscite (legal) with the remilitarisation of the Rhineland (illegal)
Don't say appeasement was 'stupid' - explain it was a reasonable policy given the circumstances
Never say Britain and France 'did nothing' - they tried diplomacy and economic pressure first
Students mix up the Nazi-Soviet Pact with the Anti-Comintern Pact
examinerTips: This topic appears in Paper 1 almost every year - focus on causation and consequences
Learn specific dates: 1936 Rhineland, 1938 Anschluss and Munich, 1939 Prague and Poland
Always explain HOW each event made war more likely, don't just describe what happened
For 'How far' questions, consider short-term AND long-term factors
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Timeline 1933-1939 showing Hitler's aggressive actions and Western responses, demonstrating the escalation from diplomatic protests to war declaration
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to explain why the policy of appeasement failed to maintain peace in Europe by 1939
In O/N 2022 P1 Q3 (6 marks) — candidates had to describe the events that led to the outbreak of World War II in September 1939
M/J 2021 P2 Q5 (10 marks) — students were required to assess whether Hitler's foreign policy was the main reason for the collapse of international peace by 1939
O/N 2020 P1 Q4 (4 marks) — the question focused on explaining why the Munich Agreement of 1938 failed to prevent further German expansion
M/J 2019 P2 Q3 (8 marks) — candidates had to explain how the failure of the League of Nations contributed to the breakdown of international peace

workedExample.question: Explain why the policy of appeasement failed to maintain peace in Europe. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define appeasement and identify the main appeasing powers (Britain and France)
Step 2: Explain the first reason - appeasement encouraged Hitler's aggression by showing weakness
Step 3: Provide specific examples (Rhineland 1936, Anschluss 1938, Sudetenland 1938)
Step 4: Explain the second reason - appeasement allowed Germany to grow stronger militarily and economically
Step 5: Show how each concession made the next demand harder to resist
Step 6: Conclude with the final failure - invasion of Poland despite guarantees
workedExample.answer: Appeasement was the policy of giving in to Hitler's demands to avoid war, primarily followed by Britain and France. It failed because it encouraged rather than deterred German aggression. When Hitler remilitarized the Rhineland in 1936, the lack of response showed him that the Western powers would not act. This emboldened him to pursue Anschluss with Austria in 1938 and then demand the Sudetenland. Each success made Hitler stronger and more confident. The Munich Agreement gave him valuable industrial resources and strategic territory, while also proving that Britain and France would sacrifice smaller nations. By 1939, Germany was too powerful to contain, and Hitler felt confident enough to invade Poland despite British and French guarantees, finally forcing them to declare war.
workedExample.markScheme: Marks awarded for: clear definition of appeasement (1-2 marks), explanation of how it encouraged aggression with examples (3-4 marks), analysis of how Germany grew stronger (2-3 marks), conclusion linking to war outbreak (1-2 marks)

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the sequence of events leading to war' — always learn the chronological order: Rhineland (1936), Anschluss (1938), Munich Agreement (1938), Prague (March 1939), Poland (September 1939)

Examiner Report 2022: 'Weak responses focused only on Hitler without mentioning the failure of collective security' — always discuss both aggressive dictators AND the weakness of democratic responses including League of Nations failures

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates often gave narrative accounts rather than analysis' — don't just describe what happened, always explain WHY each event made war more likely and HOW it contributed to the collapse of peace
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw a timeline from 1935-1939 showing the escalating crisis: Start with Italian invasion of Abyssinia (1935), then German remilitarization of Rhineland (1936), Spanish Civil War begins (1936), Anschluss with Austria (March 1938), Munich Agreement/Sudetenland crisis (September 1938), German occupation of Prague (March 1939), Nazi-Soviet Pact (August 1939), and German invasion of Poland (September 1939). Use arrows to show how each event weakened international law and encouraged further aggression. Mark in red the key turning points where decisive action could have prevented escalation.
note_data.quickRecap: International peace collapsed by 1939 due to aggressive expansion by Hitler, Mussolini and Japan, combined with the weakness of democratic powers and failure of the League of Nations. Appeasement encouraged rather than deterred aggression, allowing dictators to grow stronger with each success. Key escalation points were the Rhineland remilitarization (1936), Munich Agreement (1938), and occupation of Prague (1939), culminating in the invasion of Poland and outbreak of World War II.
